coredata.dc:title: MBT-functionalized carbon dots for enhanced water-based lubrication: Superior friction reduction and corrosion inhibition

Water-based lubricant additive
Tribological performance
Corrosion inhibition

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Mechanism of N,S-CDs(MBT) for anti-friction and anti-corrosion

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) The TEM image (insides: the particle size distribution), (b) the HR-TEM image and (c) the AFM image (insides: the height profile) of N,S-CDs. (d-f) The corresponding microstructure of N,S-CDs(MBT). (g) The XRD patterns of pure CDs, N,S-CDs and N,S-CDs(MBT). (h) The N 1 s spectra and (i) the S 2p spectra analyses of N,S-CDs and N,S-CDs(MBT).

(a) The CCD images of the different samples: (Ⅰ) as prepared and (Ⅱ) after centrifugation under 10,000 rpm for 20 min (concentration at 1.0 wt%). (b) The transmittance of the pure H2O, H2O+ 1.0 wt% CDs, H2O+ 1.0 wt% N,S-CDs and H2O+ 1.0 wt% N,S-CDs(MBT). The transmittance of (c) as-prepared samples and (d) after centrifugation for 20 min. (e) The Zeta potential of the different samples in H2O.

(a) The COF curves, (b) the average COF, (c) the anti-wear properties, (d) the 3D images and (e) the SEM images of different samples: (Ⅰ) pure H2O, (Ⅱ) H2O+ 1.0 wt% CDs, (Ⅲ) H2O+ 1.0 wt% N,S-CDs and (Ⅳ) H2O+ 1.0 wt% N,S-CDs(MBT) (Load: 5 N, Frequency: 15 Hz, Stroke: 1 mm and Duration: 10 min). The inside scale bar is 10 μm.

(a) The COF curves, (b) the partial enlarged details of the COF curves and (c) the average COF and the wear volume under the different frequency (3, 5, 7, 10 and 15 Hz) of H2O+ 1 wt% N,S-CDs(MBT). (d-f) The details of the tribological performance under different load (3, 5, 7, 10 and 15 N). (g) The schematic diagram of UMT linear reciprocating mode. (h) The real-time COF curves under start-stop experiment.
(a) Raman spectra, the XPS of (b) O 1 s and (c) Fe 2p3/2 of the worn surfaces lubricated by different lubricants: pure H2O, H2O+ 1.0 wt% CDs, H2O+ 1.0 wt% N,S-CDs and H2O+ 1.0 wt% N,S-CDs(MBT).

Characterization of the 304 steel surfaces after EIS test in (a) the OM images and (b) the SEM images: (Ⅰ) 0.1 M HCl, (Ⅱ) 0.1 M HCl+ 500 mg/L CDs, (Ⅲ) 0.1 M HCl+ 500 mg/L N,S-CDs and (Ⅳ) 0.1 M HCl+ 500 mg/L N,S-CDs(MBT). The results of (c) the Raman spectra, (d) the graphitization degree and (e) the intensity analysis of the corresponding components.
The mechanism of friction-reducing and anti-corrosion for N,S-CDs(MBT).

head.ce:title: MBT-functionalized carbon dots for enhanced water-based lubrication: Superior friction reduction and corrosion inhibition

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory of Solidification Processing, Center of Advanced Lubrication and Seal Materials, School of Materials Science and Engineering, Northwestern Polytechnical University, Xi'an 710072, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Solidification Processing, Center of Advanced Lubrication and Seal Materials, School of Materials Science and Engineering, Northwestern Polytechnical University

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: State Key Laboratory of Solidification Processing, Center of Advanced Lubrication and Seal Materials, School of Materials Science and Engineering, Northwestern Polytechnical University, Xi'an, 710072, PR China

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Energy consumption during friction has long been a critical challenge [1]. Lubrication, a key method for reducing friction and wear in mechanical equipment [2], is classified into four types: solid, semi-solid, liquid, and gaseous lubricants [3]. Among these, liquid lubrication stands out for its high thermal conductivity, prolonged effective duration, and noise-reduction, making it essential for friction and wear mitigation [4]. Liquid lubrication is further divided into oil-based and water-based types. Water-based lubricant offers distinct advantages due to the abundance, environmental compatibility, and versatile solvent properties of H2O [5]. However, their application is hindered by several challenges. Under heavy loads, high speeds and frequent start-stop conditions, the low viscosity and limited film-forming capabilities of H2O hinder the formation of a robust lubricating film, leading to potential lubrication failure [6]. Additionally, its oxidizing nature and high dielectric constant increase the risk of surfaces corrosion [7]. To overcome these challenges, it is essential to develop effective multifunctional aqueous lubricant additives capable of comprehensively improving the performance of lubricants.

Water-based lubricating additives are mainly classified into long-chain fatty acids and their derivatives [8], water-soluble high-molecular-weight polymers [9], ionic liquids [10], and nanoparticles [11]. Among these, carbon nanomaterials (CNMs) stand out due to their environmental compatibility, stable mechanical and thermal properties, and structural designability [12]. In the field of tribology, CNMs exhibit good lubrication properties through synergistic size effects (rolling, mending and polishing) and protective film effects, thus effectively reducing the coefficient of friction (COF) [13]. Berman et al. [14] achieved macroscale superlubricity (COF < 0.005) on hydrogenated diamond-like carbon (H-DLC) surfaces by combining three dimensional (3D) nanodiamonds with two-dimensional (2D) layered MoS2, attributing this effect to the formation of nano-onion carbon-rich tribological films. Additionally, the abundant reactive sites on CNMs allow for functional groups grafting and achieving multifunctional lubrication. Bohm et al. [15] prepared surface-functionalized 2D graphene by liquid-phase exfoliation and deposited submicron graphene coatings on stainless steels (SS 304 L and SS 316 L), decreasing their corrosion rates by approximately 99.97 %. Thus, CNMs are promising candidates for realizing multifunctional lubrication. However, achieving excellent lubrication performance requires the stable dispersion of CNMs in the lubricant medium. The large specific surface area and high surface energy make CNMs prone to aggregation within the lubricant, significantly impairing their tribological performance [16,17].

Carbon dots (CDs) are novel zero-dimensional (0D) CNMs with ultra-small particle sizes (< 10 nm) [18,19]. Their definitely nanoscale dimensions and tunable chemical reactivity can significantly enhance the tribological performance of lubricants [20,21]. Wang et al. [22] achieved a 77 % reduction in the wear rate of base oil PEG and macroscopic superlubricity on AISI52100 steel surfaces by utilizing carbon quantum dots (CQDs) nanofluids prepared via one-step pyrolysis. On the one hand, heteroatom-doping (non-metal and metal doping) further improves the lubricating properties of CDs. Liu et al. [23] incorporated B/N co-doped CDs into the base oil PEG and achieved a 62.18 % reduction in the COF at the addition of 1 wt%. Similarly, Tao et al. [24] introduced metal elements (Zn, Cu, Mg, Fe, U) into carbon cores skeleton of CDs, enhancing the load-carrying ability of water-based lubricants to 500 N from 120 N. In addition, surface modification of CDs has also been explored in tribology. Wang et al. [25] successfully modified the surface of CDs with an ionic liquid (NTf2 −), reducing the COF of PEG200 by 70 %. Beyond lubrication, CDs also exhibit corrosion prevention capabilities. Their rich surface functional groups enable strong adsorption onto metal surfaces through multiple anchoring actions [26], making them effective in both liquid corrosion inhibitors [27] and solid coatings [28]. Recent studies have focused on improving CDs dispersibility in various solvents through precursor selection and reaction condition optimization [29]. Qu et al. [30] prepared CDs with good water dispersibility by a heat-induced self-foaming process, using perylene-3,9-dicarboxylic acid (PDA) as the carbon source combined with boric acid and urea as assistants. Excellent dispersion of CDs in water is demonstrated by a PLQY of up to 97.2 % in the medium. Therefore, by precisely controlling CD size and surface functionalization, it is expected to achieve the desirable friction reduction performance and corrosion resistance in water-based lubrication systems.

In this study, we prepared functionalized CDs (N,S-CDs(MBT)) via one-step. By orderly controlling the reaction conditions, the obtained N,S-CDs(MBT) showed satisfying water re-dispersibility. We systematically investigated the effects of additive concentration, applied load, and working frequency on their tribological behavior as a water-based lubricant additive using a UMT tribometer. Post-friction surface analysis employing SEM, XPS and Raman spectroscopy helped us to understand the friction reduction and wear resistance mechanisms of N,S-CDs(MBT). Additionally, the anti-corrosion behavior of N,S-CDs(MBT) was assessed using the CHI760E electrochemical workstation. The results showed that the N,S-CDs(MBT) hold significant potential as a multifunctional water-based lubricant additive.

Acetaldehyde (35 wt% aqueous solution) was obtained from Aladdin Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. 2-mercaptobenzothiazole (MBT, 98 %), Thiourea (AR), HCl (AR), and NaOH (GR) were products of Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. During the CDs purification process, a Jielepu dry-type semipermeable membrane (MD77–3500) was employed. For the friction experiments, the steel disks were made of 304 stainless steel (ø24 mm × 8 mm), and the upper balls were Si3N4 ceramic balls (ø8 mm). In the anti-corrosion performance tests, the 304 steel was machined into a size of ø15 mm × 5 mm for convenient testing.

The preparation of N,S-CDs(MBT) was carried out through the following steps [31,32]. First, 11.7 g of MBT (0.07 mol) was dispersed in 20 mL of acetaldehyde solution under stirring. Next, 5 mL of NaOH aqueous solution (0.15 mol) was added dropwise. After reacting for 2 h, 400 mL HCl solution (0.15 mol) was added to neutralize the system. The resulting solid residue was then ultrasonicated in 500 mL H2O in a beaker until the yellow precipitate turned flocculent. Aggregates were removed by centrifugation and filtration using 0.22 μm water-filtering membrane. The supernatant was dialyzed in a beaker with 2000 mL of water until the external water reached neutral. Finally, the product was freeze-dried to collect N,S-CDs(MBT). For comparison, N,S-CDs were prepared using a similar procedure, substituting MBT with thiourea (0.07 mol, 5.3 g). Pure CDs (without N/S doped) were obtained from the reaction of acetaldehyde with NaOH under the same conditions.

As shown in Scheme 1, by carefully controlling the reaction conditions, the precursors acetaldehyde and MBT undergo continuous dehydration, cross-linking, and carbonization under alkaline condition. During this process, incomplete carbonization often results in the retention polymer structures on the surface and within the carbon cores [33]. Ultimately, N,S-CDs(MBTs) are formed with a carbon core that is integrated with polymer chains and abundant functional groups on their surface.

First, TEM was used to characterize the micro-morphology of the obtained samples. In Fig. 1a, N,S-CDs with thiourea as the doping agent have a broad size distribution, with some agglomerates and a lateral size concentrated around 4.26 nm. The HR-TEM image (Fig. 1b) shows no obvious crystalline characteristics. In Fig. 1c, the AFM-measured height is less than 10 nm, consistent with the typical size range of CDs [34]. In contrast, N,S-CDs(MBT) with MBT as the doping agent have a significantly smaller and more uniform size (d = 1.33 ± 0.01 nm), as observed in both TEM and AFM (Fig. 1d-f). The HR-TEM results for these CDs reveal distinct lattice fringes. Pure CDs exhibit larger sizes but still fall within the typical size range of CDs (Fig. S1). Fig. 1g presents the XRD patterns of the prepared CDs. Both pure CDs and N,S-CDs show broad peaks near 20°, characteristic of amorphous structures [35]. Upon MBT introduction, the peak intensity increases, indicating enhanced crystallinity. The observed peak shift to smaller angles might be due to an increase in the graphite layer spacing within the carbon core caused by MBT [36]. Furthermore, XPS and FT-IR were employed to analyze the chemical states of characteristic elements. Figs. S2–3 show that no significant N or S signals were detected in pure CDs. In contrast, the N 1 s signal of N,S-CDs(MBT) (Fig. 1h) displays a peak at 399.0 eV, corresponding to pyridinic-N. While in N,S-CDs, N is predominantly present as amide groups (399.7 eV) on the carbon core surface [37]. The S 2p signal of N,S-CDs(MBT) shows a peak at 162.9 eV, corresponding to C−S. For N,S-CDs prepared with thiourea, a typical sulfonate groups appears at 167.5 eV (Fig. 1i) [38]. FT-IR results display no significant differences among the three types of CDs. For example, the vibration absorption bands at 2850–3000 cm−1 are originated from C−H [39], the signal near 1674 cm−1 confirms the existence of CO [40]. With the introduction of dopants, the −OH characteristic peak near 3400 cm−1 gradually increases, indicating that N,S-CDs(MBT) have better hydrophilicity (Fig. S4) [41].

The stable dispersion of nanomaterials in lubricants is crucial for their effective performance [16]. The dispersion stability of CDs in water was evaluated by monitoring transmittance changes [42]. CDs solutions at 1.0 wt% were prepared, and their transmittance was determined using a UV-Vis. Then, the samples underwent centrifugation at 10,000 rpm for 20 min, and the transmittance of the resulting supernatant was remeasured. A smaller change in transmittance indicates a greater dispersion stability. As shown in Fig. 2a-d, after high-speed centrifugation, the transmittance of pure CDs and N,S-CDs solutions increase noticeably, while the transmittance of N,S-CDs(MBT) solution remains nearly unchanged, indicating superior dispersion stability. From Fig. S5, the low water contact angles of all three kinds of CDs indicate strong hydration ability. As shown in Fig. 2e, the Zeta potential results display that all the CDs have a significant negative charge on their surface. Notably, N,S-CDs(MBT) exhibit the highest potential at −43.0 mV. Generally, a higher Zeta potential value suggests a stronger electrostatic repulsion between colloidal particles, indicating a more stable colloidal solution [43]. The Zeta Potential results suggest that the aqueous solution containing N,S-CDs(MBT) has the strongest stability among the three samples.

The loading concentration of nanomaterial additives plays a crucial role in the tribological performance of lubricants [25]. Tribological tests were conducted at different concentration of N,S-CDs(MBT) using a UMT tribometer. As shown in Fig. S6, at low concentrations (0.1 wt%), the COF curve is unstable and shows no significant improvement compared to pure H2O. This may be ascribed to the inability of low-concentration N,S-CDs(MBT) to form an effective lubricating layer on the frictional surface. At loadings of 0.5, 1.0, and 2.0 wt%, all samples achieve low COF values with stable curves. This indicates that N,S-CDs(MBT) can form a stable lubricating film at these concentrations, effectively reducing friction and wear. Considering both lubrication stability and cost-effectiveness, 1.0 wt% was chosen as the optimal concentration. Furthermore, the tribological properties of different samples were compared under constant load and frequency (5 N, 15 Hz, 1 mm and 10 min). As shown in Fig. 3a, the COF values of lubricants containing functionalized CDs are significantly reduced, remaining below 0.2 during the test. In contrast, samples with N,S-CDs(MBT) have the shortest run-in period during which the COF remains below 0.1, decreasing from 0.474 (pure H2O) to 0.092 on average COF (Fig. 3b). Fig. 3c illustrates that the wear volume and surface roughness (Ra) of the wear scar follow a similar trend to the average COF. Although N,S-CDs increase wear volume and Ra compared to pure CDs, they still perform better than pure H2O. Notably, N,S-CDs(MBT) exhibit the best friction-reduction and wear-resistance, with the wear volume decreasing from 29.81 × 105 μm3 (pure H2O) to 7.65 × 105 μm3, as observed by a 3D optical profiler (Fig. 3d). In Fig. 3e, the surface lubricated by pure H2O shows obvious plough marks and corrosion, indicative of abrasive and corrosive wear [38]. However, when functionalized CDs are used, these plough marks become shallower and the corrosion pits smaller. Notably, the surface lubricated with N,S-CDs(MBT) shows hardly any significant corrosion wear, demonstrating that N,S-CDs(MBT) not only enhance tribological performance but also effectively mitigate the corrosive effects of pure H2O on the worn surface.

Furthermore, we assessed the tribological performance of N,S-CDs(MBT) under different frequencies and varying loads. As shown in Fig. 4a-c, the COF values of the samples decrease gradually with increasing test frequency, and the run-in period becomes shorter. The average COF also decreases stepwise as test frequency rises. In contrast to the frequency-dependent test results, under increasing load, the COF of all samples remains stable, with the average COF below 0.10 (Fig. 4d-f). This highlights the strong load-adaptability of N,S-CDs(MBT). In terms of the wear volume, it increases with higher frequency and load. During the operation of equipment, frequent start-stop cycles are very common [44]. To simulate this situation, 60 μL of lubricant containing N,S-CDs(MBT) was added between the friction pairs. Under constant load, frequency, and stroke, six consecutive start-stop cycles were conducted, with COF values recorded simultaneously. During the stops, the relative position of the friction pairs was maintained, and no additional lubricant was added. As shown in Fig. 4g-h, during the six continuous cycles, the COF rises initially but quickly stabilizes, remaining below 0.120 throughout the test. This demonstrates that N,S-CDs(MBT), as a water-based lubricant additive, can effectively adapt to varying frequency and load conditions. Its stable performance under frequent start-stop operations highlights its strong reliability in practical applications. Meanwhile, the long-term tribological test (5 N, 15 Hz, 1 mm and 1 h) further demonstrates the potential of the functionalized CDs for long-term service applications (Fig. S7).

Surface composition after lubrication with different samples was thoroughly analyzed via Raman spectroscopy and XPS. As shown in Fig. 5a, only the surface lubricated by N,S-CDs(MBT) exhibits weak carbon peaks (1350 cm−1-D band; 1580 cm−1-G band), with signals near 200–700 cm−1 attributed to iron oxide components [45]. This is mainly because N,S-CDs(MBT) has a higher degree of graphitization, which deposits on the surfaces of friction pairs, making the characteristic carbon signals more evident. Moreover, the stronger negative charge of N,S-CDs(MBT) facilitates its adsorption on the positively charged friction surface [46]. Unfortunately, XPS failed to detect significant characteristic N/S signals (Fig. S8). This is consistent with our previous FIB observations, which indicates that the tribo-protective film typically forms a bilayer structure [47]. During friction, active element S initially reacts with the substrate to form metal sulfides in the subsurface layer adjacent to the friction pairs, while the surface of the friction pairs usually forms an amorphous carbon film due to the subsequent deposition of CDs [38]. As a result, no significant N/S signals were observed on the surface. In Fig. S9, all spectra were calibrated using the hydrocarbon pollution carbon (284.8 eV) as a benchmark. As shown in Fig. 5b, three characteristic absorption peaks in the O 1 s region are identified at 530.1 (metal carbide), 532.0 (CO), and 533.7 eV (C−O). In contrast, the worn surfaces based on N,S-CDs and N,S-CDs(MBT) exhibit more pronounced peaks corresponding to metal oxide. From the Fe 2p3/2 region, significant peaks at 710.4 and 713.7 eV are attributed to iron oxides and iron sulfides, respectively. Metal carbide signals (707.2 eV) are also detected on surfaces lubricated by N,S-CDs and N,S-CDs(MBT) (Fig. 5c). The characteristic Si signal originates from the upper friction pairs, corresponding to the Si3N4 component (Fig. S10). The above results show that, under continuous longitudinal pressure and transverse shear force, a tribo-protective film mainly composed of metal carbides, metal oxides and metal sulfides forms at the interface of friction pairs. Additionally, the effective adsorption of N,S-CDs(MBT) on the substrate facilitates the formation of carbon-based tribo-films. These two mechanisms work together to notably reduce friction and wear.

Water-based lubrication can often lead to severe corrosion of friction pairs. To explore the potential corrosion inhibition of N,S-CDs(MBT), 304 steels were immersed in four different solutions: 0.1 M HCl, 0.1 M HCl+ 500 mg/L CDs, 0.1 M HCl+ 500 mg/L N,S-CDs and 0.1 M HCl+ 500 mg/L N,S-CDs(MBT). Corrosion behavior was assessed using Open Circuit Potential (OCP) and Tafel curves. Fig. 6 presents the OCP curves and Tafel curves for 304 steels in various solutions at room temperature. After 48 h of immersion, all OCP curves are stabilized. The addition of pure CDs leads to a negative shift in OCP, while N,S-CDs have a minimal impact. However, N,S-CDs(MBT) significantly shift the OCP in the positive direction, indicating a potential anodic half-reaction corrosion inhibition (Fig. 6a) [48]. As shown in Fig. 6b and Table S1, the E
corr and i
corr were determined by the Tafel extrapolation method [49]. The results reveal that both pure CDs and N,S-CDs provide negligible corrosion protection for 304 steels. However, the addition of N,S-CDs(MBT) markedly reduces the current density of both the anodic and cathodic branches of the polarization curves, suggesting that N,S-CDs(MBT) inhibits the anodic and cathodic reactions. The corrosion inhibition efficiency was evaluated by comparing the i
corr values, as shown in Fig. 6c. The addition of CDs increases i
corr , while N,S-CDs reduce i
corr to some extent but with a low inhibition efficiency (12.12 %). In contrast, N,S-CDs(MBT) dramatically decrease i
corr from 25.00 mA/cm2 to 0.32 mA/cm2, achieving a high inhibition efficiency of 98.7 %. This significant improvement can be on account of the formation of a protective film on the substrate by N,S-CDs(MBT), which effectively shields the substrate from corrosive agents [26]. As shown in Fig. 6d-g, a 3D profilometer was used to obtain the surface information of the steel disk after the corrosion test. With the addition of samples, both surface roughness and vertical height fluctuations gradually decrease. The most significant improvement is observed with N,S-CDs(MBT), which reduce the surface roughness from 0.358 μm to 0.026 μm and the vertical height fluctuation from 1.17 μm to 0.09 μm.

As shown in Fig. 7a-b, the steel disks surfaces after corrosion testing were further examined in detail using OM and SEM. In the absence of CDs, the steel surface exhibits severe corrosion with a rough surface and numerous corrosion-induced micropores. The introduction of CDs and N,S-CDs mitigates the corrosion to some extent. Notably, the addition of 500 mg/L N,S-CDs(MBT) provides substantial protection for the steel surface, resulting in a smooth surface with only a few isolated corrosion micropores. EDS mappings reveal that these micropores and surface traces mainly corresponded to the precipitation of carbides and Si elements (Fig. S11). As shown in Fig. 7c-e, Raman spectroscopy reveals the presence of iron oxide (550 cm−1) on surfaces treated with N,S-CDs(MBT). The D peak near 1340 cm−1 indicates disordered carbon, while the G peak around 1541 cm−1 originates from sp2-C vibrations. The absorption at 1440 cm−1 corresponds to the sp3 phase within disordered carbon [50]. These spectral features observed suggest the emergence of a carbon-rich protective film. In brief, N,S-CDs(MBT) act as effective anodic corrosion inhibitors by forming a disordered carbon-based protective layer on the steel surface, efficiently suppressing corrosion on friction pairs surface [45].

As shown in Table S2, the functionalized CDs developed in this work exhibit marked tribological performance compared to other types of aqueous additives. Meanwhile, in comparison to commercial corrosion inhibitors (benzotriazole (BTA) and MBT), N,S-CDs(MBT) demonstrate enhanced corrosion resistance (Fig. S12).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Mechanism of N,S-CDs(MBT) for anti-friction and anti-corrosion
ce:section.ce:para: Based on the above analysis, the friction-reduction and corrosion-resistance mechanisms of N,S-CDs(MBT) can be summarized in Scheme 2. First, N,S-CDs(MBT) have abundant hydrophilic functional groups (e.g., −OH, −COOH) on their surface, endowing them with strong hydration ability. This property enables their stable dispersion in water-based lubricants, preventing aggregation and facilitating the formation of a stable hydration layer during friction (Fig. S13). The presence of this stable hydration layer is beneficial for the generation of a robust boundary lubricating film, which plays a critical role in reducing wear. Second, the negatively charged N,S-CDs(MBT) can adsorb onto the frictional surface through electrostatic adsorption. Under the combined action of external oxygen and frictional heat, the active N and S element of N,S-CDs(MBT) can react with the substrate to form a tribological protective film. Simultaneously, the continuous application of lateral shear force and vertical pressure during friction promotes the deposition of N,S-CDs(MBT), resulting in the development of an amorphous carbon film, and effectively reducing wear. Meanwhile, N,S-CDs(MBT) as an anodic corrosion inhibitor, their heteroatoms (e.g., N, S) possess lone pair electrons capable of forming coordination bonds with the empty 3D orbitals of Fe atoms on the steel surface. This interaction promotes the enhanced adsorption of N,S-CDs(MBT) onto the metal substrate, effectively mitigating corrosion during water-based lubrication [26]. In summary, the synergistic effect of the tribo-protective film, amorphous carbon deposition, and hydration layer formation endows N,S-CDs(MBT) with friction-reduction and wear-resistance capabilities. Moreover, the anti-corrosion effect of N,S-CDs(MBT) provides a comprehensive solution to the corrosion challenges commonly encountered in water-based lubrication.

In summary, this work has functionalized CDs via a one-step hydroxyl-aldehyde condensation at room temperature. As a water-based lubricant additive, N,S-CDs(MBT) exhibit satisfying water re-dispersibility, friction-reduction and wear-resistance. At a concentration of 1.0 wt%, they reduce the average COF of pure H2O by 80.6 % and decrease wear volume by 74.3 %. This improvement is mainly attributed to the combined influence of the tribo-protective film, amorphous carbon film, and hydration layer, which together form a stable boundary lubricating film to mitigate friction and wear. Additionally, N,S-CDs(MBT) demonstrate marked corrosion inhibition. At just 500 mg/L, they can reduce the corrosion current density i
corr from 25.00 mA/cm2 to 0.32 mA/cm2, significantly mitigating corrosion in water-based lubrication. These properties highlight N,S-CDs(MBT) as a promising multifunctional water-based lubricant additive, offering valuable insights for the development of multifunctional lubricants.
The authors declare that this manuscript is an original work, and the manuscript or part of it has not been published or submitted elsewhere.

Qian Ye: Writing – review & editing, Funding acquisition. Shenghua Xue: Data curation. Tiantian Wang: Data curation. Bin Liu: Formal analysis. Guangkai Jin: Writing – original draft, Formal analysis, Data curation. Shujuan Liu: Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (52475215, U23A20623), the Western Light Project of CAS (xbzg-zdsys-202118), and the Innovation Foundation for Doctor Dissertation of Northwestern Polytechnical University (CX2025052).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.triboint.2025.110854.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Global energy consumption due to friction and wear in the mining industry

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Holmberg K., Kivikytö-Reponen P., Härkisaari P., Valtonen K., Erdemir A. Global energy consumption due to friction and wear in the mining industry. Tribo Int. 2017;115:116-139.
Zhou Z. Tribology in China. Friction. 2025;13:9440994.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Lubricating a bright future: lubrication contribution to energy saving and low carbon emission

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Fabrication of zwitterionic polymer-functionalized onion-like carbon nanoparticles as aqueous lubricant additives
